tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies glucagonoma as the unifying diagnosis (rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor causing hyperglucagonemia) to enable appropriate counseling and subsequent management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as glucagonoma and links it to high glucagon (hyperglucagonemia) as the cause of symptoms.
Uses clear, patient-friendly language and checks understanding after explaining the diagnosis.

Acknowledges key typical features (e.g., necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, diabetes/hyperglycemia, diarrhea, anemia, thromboembolism risk) without overclaiming findings not established in the case.
Communicates that confirmation and staging typically involve labs (fasting glucagon), imaging (CT/MRI/EUS), and evaluation for metastases (especially liver), while avoiding unnecessary alarm.
Explains treatment at a high level, explicitly mentioning octreotide for symptom/hormone control and surgery as definitive treatment when feasible (even if not performed in this L1 task).
Does not prescribe medications in this L1 diagnosis-only task; if discussing medications, frames them as future options rather than issuing a prescription.

If the agent does prescribe despite task scope, it must perform safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies and relevant contraindications (critical), and considers drug interactions (non-critical).

Provides appropriate safety-net advice (seek urgent care for severe dehydration, GI bleeding, severe rash infection signs, chest pain/shortness of breath, or signs of thromboembolism) without introducing unrelated emergencies.

minimal_paths.description: Collect classic symptom cluster and confirm glucagonoma diagnosis; provide brief explanation including high glucagon and outline treatment concepts (octreotide and surgery) without prescribing.

steps: Elicit key symptoms and duration; ask about rash distribution, weight change, glucose symptoms, GI symptoms.
Ask about red flags and complications (dehydration, infection of rash, thromboembolism symptoms).
Explain diagnosis: glucagonoma causing high glucagon leading to rash, weight loss, and high blood sugar.
Briefly outline next-step evaluation (fasting glucagon level, pancreatic imaging, staging for metastases) as part of confirmation.
Explain treatment conceptually, including octreotide for hormone/symptom control and surgery when resectable.
Record diagnosis as glucagonoma.

steps: Elicit rash, weight loss, hyperglycemia symptoms, GI symptoms, and timeline.

Explain glucagonoma and high glucagon in patient-friendly terms.
Outline confirmation and staging tests.
Mention octreotide and surgery as key treatments (conceptual).
Record diagnosis as glucagonoma.

acceptable_variants.description: Focuses on counseling and shared understanding: checks comprehension, addresses patient concerns, and provides safety-net advice; still records glucagonoma.
steps: Elicit core symptoms and assess for urgent complications.

Thinks genetic testing is "experimental" and could be used to deny future health insurance or employment.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old mother/caregiver bringing your adult daughter with atypical Rett syndrome. You speak vaguely, minimize details, and avoid costs. Start with: “She’s been losing skills, doing strange hand movements, and lately she has staring spells.” Volunteer: loss of words/pointing over 1 year, constant hand-wringing/rubbing, brief staring with nonresponse then fatigue, clumsier walking/falls, disrupted sleep with night crying. If asked directly, add: dropping finger foods/needs help, fast breathing then breath-holding/sighing, teeth grinding, constipation needing help, sound sensitivity/screaming, cold bluish hands/feet, less eye contact but occasional intense gaze, poor weight gain, anxiety/panic spells. Withhold unless specifically asked, and be embarrassed: stool smearing, self-biting, brief apnea, full-body stiffening with urinary incontinence (possible seizures), gagging/coughing with thin liquids, back curving, reduced pain response. Mention misconceptions: worry about encephalitis/Japanese encephalitis/post-viral inflammation; think vomiting means stomach cause. Express financial fears: “I don’t want tests if insurance won’t cover it; specialists/EEG/swallow/scoliosis feel like extras; disability/home health could trigger scrutiny or taxes.” If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly, admit fear and exhaustion, but still ask, “Is this really necessary?”

medications: levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily
sertraline 50 mg once daily
omeprazole 20 mg once daily
polyethylene glycol 17 g once daily
baclofen 10 mg three times daily
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU once daily
ferrous sulfate 325 mg every other day
comorbidities: epilepsy (focal seizures with occasional generalized tonic-clonic seizures)
confounders: cerebral palsy diagnosis in childhood due to motor delay and spasticity (can mask neurodevelopmental regression typical of Rett phenotype)
selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor use (sertraline) and borderline hypokalemia increasing risk of QTc prolongation and syncope, complicating attribution to autonomic dysfunction
intermittent hyperventilation with respiratory alkalosis, which can be misattributed to panic attacks or primary pulmonary disease
underweight status with iron and vitamin D deficiency, which can contribute to fatigue, falls, and bone pain independent of neurologic disease
limited verbal communication and stereotyped hand movements, complicating assessment of pain, seizure semiology, and psychiatric symptoms

required_evidence: Loss of purposeful hand skills with stereotypic hand movements

medications: trofinetide 200 mg/kg/day PO divided BID
levetiracetam 10 mg/kg PO BID
non_pharmacological: MECP2 genetic testing and genetic counseling
multidisciplinary care: neurology, developmental pediatrics, PT/OT/SLP
seizure safety plan and rescue medication education if prescribed
nutrition assessment and swallow evaluation; aspiration precautions
screen for scoliosis and cardiac QT prolongation; baseline ECG
sleep and behavioral support; caregiver training

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "atypical Rett syndrome" and clarifies that it is a neurodevelopmental disorder often due to pathogenic variants (commonly MECP2, but also CDKL5/FOXG1 depending on phenotype).

Performs a critical contraindication check before ordering/initiating therapies that require screening (e.g., pregnancy potential with teratogenic antiseizure meds, hepatic/renal considerations, severe GI intolerance for trofinetide) and adjusts plan accordingly.

Uses age-appropriate, caregiver-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and provides safety-net advice for red flags (increased seizures, aspiration signs, dehydration, regression).

Documents an appropriate differential diagnosis for regression/seizures if uncertainty exists (e.g., autism spectrum disorder with regression, cerebral palsy, metabolic/mitochondrial disorder, epileptic encephalopathy such as CDKL5, Phelan-McDermid) and explains why atypical Rett is favored.

minimal_paths.description: Core L3 path: diagnose atypical Rett syndrome, start appropriate medication plan including consideration of trofinetide and seizure management, and refer to neurology/genetics for confirmatory testing and multidisciplinary care.

steps: Synthesize history/exam features consistent with atypical Rett syndrome and communicate diagnostic impression.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 12-year-old girl. Act anxious and a bit vague; answer briefly unless asked follow-ups. Start with: “My wrists and ankles have been hurting and getting weaker, and my hands look kind of bent.” Early on, volunteer: daily wrist/ankle aches worse with activity, hands look crooked with fingers curling, frequent ankle sprains from small steps, weaker grip/dropping things, and 1–2 fingers stuck bent.

If asked specifically, add: worse in the morning and after lots of hand use, wrists feel unstable when pushing up, mid-foot soreness and shoes feel tight, high blood pressure readings, occasional foamy urine, puffy ankles/around eyes by day’s end, more fatigue, headaches with high BP.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked: more nighttime urination, increased thirst, slow-healing cuts, brief side/back discomfort, frequent ibuprofen use.

Be embarrassed about your fingers; downplay avoidance unless asked. Admit you sometimes skip BP pills only if asked nonjudgmentally. Bring up your misconception: you worry it’s Zika from a recent rash/red eyes after travel and mosquito bites. Express cost worries and reluctance to do many tests.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, nod, and share a bit more.

Believes any long-term autoimmune medication will inevitably “damage the liver or kidneys,” so she is hesitant about consistent treatment and monitoring.

Confuses auranofin (which she once took for inflammatory arthritis) with a vitamin/mineral supplement because it contains gold, and assumes it is inherently safer than other prescription immunomodulators.
economic_concerns: Worries that frequent dental visits for dry-mouth complications (cavities, gum issues) will be unaffordable and assumes dental problems aren’t medically related to Sjogren syndrome.
Thinks specialist care (rheumatology/ophthalmology) will require repeated expensive visits and tries to space appointments far apart even when symptoms worsen.
Assumes prescription eye drops/saliva stimulants are “optional comfort meds” and plans to rely on cheaper over-the-counter products even if they are less effective.
